## RL Applications in Trading Systems

The application of reinforcement learning to financial trading has evolved from
theoretical proposals to practical implementations across diverse market
segments. This section surveys the literature on RL-based trading systems with
a focus on methodology, action space design, and evaluation rigor.

To keep conclusions methodologically defensible, this review distinguishes
between: (i) proof-of-concept evidence from backtests under simplified
assumptions, and (ii) stronger evidence that includes robust out-of-sample
design, realistic cost modeling, and variance reporting across repeated runs.

### Early Applications and Proof-of-Concept

@Moody1998 presented the first comprehensive application of RL to trading,
introducing recurrent reinforcement learning for optimizing trading systems.
His approach incorporated four design choices that remain influential: the
Differential Sharpe Ratio as a risk-adjusted reward, a recurrent network
architecture for temporal state encoding, direct policy optimization rather
than a predict-then-trade pipeline, and explicit transaction cost modeling.
Moody demonstrated that RL could outperform supervised learning baselines on
S&P 500 futures, establishing feasibility of the approach and showing that
optimizing risk-adjusted return directly was often superior to predicting
intermediate outcomes.

@Moody2001 extended this framework to multi-asset portfolios, validating that
RL-optimized policies could generate positive risk-adjusted returns after
transaction costs — a stringent test of practical viability.

### From Discrete to Continuous Action Spaces

Early value-based approaches applied Q-learning with discrete buy/sell/hold
actions to equity and futures markets [@Neuneier1998; @Lee2001]. These studies
established that RL could learn profitable patterns from historical data, but
also identified persistent problems: sparse rewards, delayed credit assignment,
and out-of-sample performance degradation. More fundamentally, discrete action
spaces force an artificial granularity on position sizing — a coarse grid
loses control precision while a fine grid reintroduces the curse of
dimensionality.

@Gold2003 and @Dempster2006 demonstrated that policy gradient methods with
continuous action spaces addressed this limitation directly. Continuous
portfolio weights proved more natural for allocation tasks and policy gradient
methods achieved better risk-adjusted returns with fewer training episodes,
establishing the case for actor-critic approaches in trading.

### Deep Reinforcement Learning Era

The introduction of deep function approximators to RL trading extended
applicability to high-dimensional state spaces.

@Xiong2018 and @Deng2016 adapted DQN for equity and cryptocurrency trading,
demonstrating that convolutional networks could extract useful features from
raw price data. However, both studies noted high variance across runs —
a characteristic instability of value-based methods under noisy financial
rewards.

@Jiang2017 proposed the portfolio-vector-memory (PVM) architecture, combining
policy gradients with recurrent networks for cryptocurrency portfolio management.
Their fully differentiable end-to-end design incorporated current holdings into
the state and penalized transaction costs explicitly, outperforming
Markowitz mean-variance allocation in backtests across 12 cryptocurrencies.

@Liang2018 applied DDPG to stock trading and conducted ablation studies showing
that: (i) DDPG outperformed DQN for continuous position sizing, (ii) Sharpe-based
rewards improved out-of-sample generalization, and (iii) transaction cost
modeling was critical for realistic performance assessment.

@Majidi2024 provided a focused TD3 study on single-asset trading for AMZN and
BTC using daily data and a continuous action space in $[-1,1]$. Their minimal
state representation — lagged close-return windows rather than large
technical-indicator sets — reported improved return and Sharpe ratio for TD3
versus discrete variants and buy-and-hold, though results were mixed across
assets. This study is best interpreted as evidence that continuous action sizing
can be beneficial under realistic frictions, not as definitive proof of robust
alpha across market regimes.

@Kabbani2022 proposed a TD3-based framework incorporating account state (cash
and holdings), price features, technical indicators, and optional FinBERT-derived
news sentiment. Richer state designs improved both return and Sharpe ratio
relative to a close-price-only baseline, with a final 10-stock evaluation
reporting a Sharpe ratio of 2.68. Methodologically the paper supports the
practical value of feature enrichment in continuous-action actor-critic trading,
while also illustrating how performance claims depend strongly on evaluation
protocol choices.

@Wang2019 and @Yang2020 demonstrated PPO in cryptocurrency and high-frequency
trading settings respectively, noting superior training stability relative to
DDPG at the cost of lower sample efficiency. @Yang2020 in particular highlighted
the scalability challenges of training stable policies on millisecond-resolution
LOB data, requiring extensive hyperparameter tuning.

@Zhang2020 integrated attention mechanisms with deep RL for stock trading,
enabling the agent to focus on relevant features dynamically. The attention
weights provided partial interpretability — visualizing which indicators
influenced trading decisions at each timestep — partially addressing the black
box critique of deep RL systems.

### Comparative Evidence and Limitations

**Algorithm comparisons.**
@Shen2021 benchmarked DQN, DDPG, PPO, TD3, and SAC across asset classes,
finding that off-policy methods (DDPG, TD3, SAC) learned faster than on-policy
alternatives, with TD3 and SAC exhibiting the lowest variance across random
seeds. No algorithm dominated across all markets; best choice depended on
reward noise and action space dimensionality.

@Lopez2018 compared deep RL against traditional quantitative strategies
(momentum, mean reversion, pairs trading), noting that RL exhibited superior
adaptability during regime changes while traditional strategies offered better
interpretability. Hybrid approaches combining domain knowledge with RL often
outperformed either in isolation.

**Overfitting and generalization.**
@Krauss2017 demonstrated that many published RL trading strategies fail to
generalize out-of-sample, attributing this to high noise-to-signal ratios in
financial data, limited training data relative to model complexity, and
non-stationarity that invalidates training-period patterns. The implication is
that evaluation methodology — strict temporal splits, multi-seed reporting,
realistic cost assumptions — matters as much as algorithm choice.

**Reward specification.**
@Cong2020 argues that reward engineering is critical and often under-examined.
Misspecified rewards can lead to metric gaming (optimizing Sharpe by avoiding
trades), ignoring tail risks, and misalignment with actual investor preferences.
The Differential Sharpe Ratio [@Moody1998] addresses some of these concerns by
optimizing risk-adjusted return directly, but does not fully resolve the
tension between reward simplicity and the richness of real trading objectives.

### Structured Comparison of Representative Studies

```{=latex}
\begingroup
\footnotesize
\setlength{\tabcolsep}{4pt}
\renewcommand{\arraystretch}{1.3}
\begin{longtable}{p{1.8cm}p{2.0cm}p{2.4cm}p{1.8cm}p{2.2cm}p{2.6cm}}
\toprule
\textbf{Study} &
\textbf{Market / Task} &
\textbf{State Variables} &
\textbf{Action Space} &
\textbf{Reward / Objective} &
\textbf{Main Caveat} \\
\midrule
\endfirsthead
\multicolumn{6}{c}{\textit{(continued from previous page)}} \\
\toprule
\textbf{Study} &
\textbf{Market / Task} &
\textbf{State Variables} &
\textbf{Action Space} &
\textbf{Reward / Objective} &
\textbf{Main Caveat} \\
\midrule
\endhead
\midrule
\multicolumn{6}{r}{\textit{(continued on next page)}} \\
\endfoot
\bottomrule
\endlastfoot

Moody \& Saffell (1998) &
Futures trading &
Price-derived signals + recurrent state &
Continuous position control &
Differential Sharpe Ratio &
Limited modern reproducibility standards \\

Jiang et al.\ (2017) &
Crypto portfolio allocation &
Price tensor + previous portfolio weights &
Continuous portfolio weights &
Commission-aware portfolio growth &
Backtest dependence on selected period and universe \\

Liang et al.\ (2018) &
Stock portfolio management &
Market features + macro variables &
Continuous (DDPG) &
Return with friction-aware setup &
Sensitivity to architecture and hyperparameters \\

Majidi et al.\ (2024) &
Single-asset AMZN/BTC &
Close-return sliding window (autoregressive) &
Continuous $[-1,1]$ &
Log-return with transaction cost &
Mixed performance vs.\ buy-and-hold across assets \\

Kabbani \& Duman (2022) &
Multi-asset stock trading &
Cash + holdings + close + indicators + sentiment &
Continuous (TD3) &
Portfolio return / Sharpe-oriented &
Sharpe likely optimistic under strict frozen-policy evaluation \\

Shen et al.\ (2021) &
Cross-algorithm benchmarking &
Task-dependent feature sets &
Mixed (discrete + continuous) &
Comparative return / risk metrics &
Result portability depends on data and protocol harmonization \\

\end{longtable}
\endgroup
```

The literature reviewed here converges on several conclusions relevant to the
design choices in this thesis. Continuous-action actor-critic methods —
particularly DDPG and TD3 — consistently outperform discrete-action
alternatives for position-sizing tasks, a finding confirmed across independent
studies with different assets and market regimes [@Liang2018; @Majidi2024;
@Kabbani2022]. Risk-adjusted reward signals, especially Differential Sharpe
Ratio formulations, tend to improve out-of-sample generalization relative to
raw return objectives, though they must be paired with evaluation on realized
financial metrics rather than the training signal itself to avoid conflating
optimization proxy with economic performance. Explicit transaction cost modeling
is a prerequisite for any realistic performance claim at the frequencies
considered here.

A consistent gap in the literature is the reliance on OHLCV or candlestick
representations as the primary state input. Studies that operate at tick
frequency and exploit the full depth of the limit order book remain scarce,
despite the theoretical case — established in the microstructure literature —
that the informational content relevant to short-horizon price prediction is
concentrated precisely in the queue dynamics and imbalance structure that OHLCV
aggregates discard. The present thesis addresses this gap directly by
constructing the agent's observation vector entirely from 10-level order book
data, using features grounded in the same theoretical frameworks reviewed in the
preceding section.
